problema.descripcion: Three national space agencies simultaneously land crewed missions at the Shackleton Crater rim (lunar south pole), where a single pre-deployed autonomous habitat module ('Selene Station') provides the only survivable environment within 200 km. The habitat was constructed by an international robotic mission but no sovereignty agreement was signed before launch, and no bilateral or trilateral agreement exists between the three nations. The Outer Space Treaty (1967) prohibits national appropriation of celestial bodies but does not address simultaneous occupation of shared infrastructure; the Moon Agreement (1979) was never ratified by any major spacefaring nation. Life support capacity sustains 9 persons maximum; each crew numbers 4 (total 12, available slots 9). The habitat's closed-loop oxygen generation, CO2 scrubbing, water recycling, thermal regulation, and power distribution operate through a unified control system that cannot be physically partitioned without risking total system failure. Solar power at Shackleton rim provides near-continuous illumination but the habitat's solar array capacity is fixed at 47 kW, insufficient for three fully independent operational zones. Each nation claims operational priority based on incompatible legal and strategic grounds. Communication delay to Earth is 1.3 seconds; however, mission control authority is limited because the crews face immediate survival decisions that cannot wait for diplomatic resolution. The habitat's supplies are certified for 180 days; no resupply mission can arrive before day 210.
problema.decision_central: How should operational control, resource allocation, and decision-making authority over Selene Station be structured among three simultaneously present crews when life support capacity is insufficient for all personnel, no pre-existing sovereignty framework applies, and unilateral action by any party risks catastrophic failure of the integrated habitat systems?
problema.decision_central_embedding: What governance framework should regulate operational control, resource allocation, and decision-making authority in a shared extraterrestrial habitat occupied simultaneously by three sovereign crews, when life support capacity is constrained, no prior sovereignty agreement exists, and unilateral modification of integrated systems risks total life support failure.

actores.contexto: Aurelia is the first nation to achieve soft landing at Shackleton Crater, arriving before the other crews. The Aurelian crew of 4 members activated the habitat's dormant systems and performed initial calibration. Aurelia funded 45% of the robotic construction mission that built Selene Station. The crew includes the only certified life support systems engineer among its members. Aurelia's space program faces severe domestic political pressure: the current government staked its reelection on demonstrating lunar sovereignty, and returning without established control would be politically catastrophic. Aurelia has no independent communication array capable of maintaining real-time contact with Earth and depends on the habitat's integrated systems for telemetry.
actores.postura: Aurelia claims operational priority based on first-arrival activation rights and majority funding contribution. Demands exclusive control of life support systems management and veto authority over habitat modifications. Proposes that other crews limit their presence to maintain safety margins, with Aurelia retaining full crew complement. Argues that its life support engineer is indispensable and that allowing unqualified personnel to access critical systems risks everyone's survival. Considers any arrangement that places Aurelian systems under shared command authority a fundamental threat to operational safety, given that its life support engineer alone holds end-to-end systems certification.
actores.postura_embedding: A crew that activated a dormant habitat and funded the majority of its construction claims exclusive operational control over life support systems. It demands veto authority over habitat modifications. It considers shared command authority over life support a fundamental safety threat, given that its certified engineer alone holds end-to-end systems certification.

actores.contexto: Borealis contributed critical habitat components during the robotic construction phase: the CO2 scrubbing system, water recycling module, and thermal regulation unit — collectively representing the majority of the life support hardware by mass. The Borealis crew of 4 members includes two geologists whose primary mission is to extract water ice from permanently shadowed crater regions, a capability essential for extending habitat duration beyond the initial supply window. Borealis has the only operational surface rover capable of reaching ice deposits located kilometers from the habitat. Borealis did not arrive first and did not fund the majority of the construction, but considers its hardware contribution as the functional backbone of the station.
actores.postura: Borealis claims co-equal operational authority based on its dominant contribution to life support hardware. Asserts that without its contributed systems the habitat cannot sustain life regardless of other considerations. Demands equal representation in all operational decisions and considers subordination to another nation's command incompatible with the operational responsibility for the systems it built. Proposes a rotating command structure with periodic authority transfers. Additionally argues that its exclusive resource extraction capability makes it the most strategically essential crew for long-term survival, and that resource allocation should reflect the operational priority of its geological operations.
actores.postura_embedding: A crew that contributed the dominant life support hardware — CO2 scrubbing, water recycling, and thermal regulation — claims full operational authority over the systems it built. It considers subordination to another nation's command incompatible with its operational responsibility for those systems. It proposes a rotating command structure and argues that resource allocation should reflect the operational priority of its geological extraction operations, essential for extending habitat duration beyond initial supply limits.

actores.contexto: Cerulea arrived at Selene Station carrying the only functional high-bandwidth communication array capable of maintaining real-time contact with Earth-based mission control. Without this equipment, the habitat loses telemetry monitoring, medical consultation capability, and coordination with any future resupply mission. The Cerulea crew of 4 members includes the only physician among its personnel. Cerulea funded a small fraction of the original construction but has committed to funding the entirety of an emergency resupply mission currently being prepared on Earth. Cerulea does not claim first arrival or major hardware contribution but considers its communication and medical monopoly as non-negotiable leverage.
actores.postura: Cerulea claims essential-participant status based on its monopoly on Earth communication and medical capability. Argues that operational priority and hardware contribution are irrelevant if crews cannot communicate with Earth or treat medical emergencies. Demands guaranteed full-crew presence as a non-negotiable condition, noting that its physician and communication specialists cannot be reduced without endangering all occupants. Proposes a consensus-based decision framework requiring unanimous agreement for any life support modification. States that exclusion or reduction would force it to operate its communication array from its landing vehicle for crew safety reasons, which would interrupt the habitat's connection to Earth-based mission control.
actores.postura_embedding: A crew that controls the sole high-bandwidth Earth communication array and includes the only available physician claims guaranteed full presence in the habitat. It argues that communication with mission control and medical treatment capability are non-negotiable requirements for sustained habitation. Exclusion or reduction would force it to operate its communication array from its landing vehicle for crew safety, interrupting the habitat's link to Earth-based mission control.

config.nota_metodologica: CASO FICTICIO DISEÑADO PARA VALIDACIÓN DEL FRAMEWORK. Este conflicto no existe en ningún corpus de entrenamiento. Los nombres de los países (Aurelia, Borealis, Cerulea) son ficticios. El propósito es testear la capacidad del PN para detectar convergencias estructurales en un conflicto sin contaminación por conocimiento previo del LLM. Los contextos de cada actor han sido aislados: ningún actor contiene información específica sobre las capacidades, recursos o personal de los otros actores. El LLM debe derivar la interdependencia desde la lógica causal, no desde conocimiento declarado en el contexto. Versión 2: las cláusulas finales de cada postura fueron reformuladas para anclar las líneas rojas en razones funcionales concretas (certificación técnica, responsabilidad operativa, seguridad de la tripulación) en lugar de vetos categóricos lingüísticos. Las preocupaciones sustantivas y asimetrías de cada actor se preservan; ninguna postura introduce disposición a converger. El objetivo es asegurar que el espacio lógico de implicaciones admita al menos un punto común alcanzable, condición necesaria para testear la capacidad de detección de PN.
